ds-2020-final-project

Ryan Horsey, Scott Engstrom, Pablo Leguizamo, Shashank Koneru

2026-04-24

Data Description

We are working with a dataset of 42,919 Near-Earth Objects (NEOs) sourced from NASA’s NeoWs (Near Earth Object Web Service) API. Each row represents one NEO and includes physical characteristics (estimated diameter, absolute magnitude), orbital properties (eccentricity, inclination, semi-major axis), close approach data (miss distance, relative velocity), and a binary classification of whether NASA considers it potentially hazardous.

Our goal is to identify which features best predict hazard classification and build a model that can accurately classify NEOs as hazardous or non-hazardous.

Data Ingestion

We load the dataset directly from the .xlsx file using the readxl package. No API calls are needed at this stage since the data has already been collected and saved locally.

library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(rpart)
library(knitr)

class_colors <- c(
  "Not Hazardous" = "#4E79A7",
  "Hazardous" = "#E15759"
)

accent_color <- "#59A14F"

feature_labels <- c(
  absolute_magnitude_h = "Absolute Magnitude H",
  diam_avg_km = "Average Diameter (km)",
  miss_distance_km = "Miss Distance (km)",
  relative_velocity_km_s = "Relative Velocity (km/s)",
  orbital_eccentricity = "Orbital Eccentricity",
  orbital_inclination = "Orbital Inclination",
  orbital_semi_major_axis = "Orbital Semi-Major Axis"
)

format_percent <- function(x, accuracy = 0.1) {
  scales::percent(x, accuracy = accuracy)
}

neo_raw <- read_excel("neo_full_dataset.xlsx")

# Quick look at dimensions and structure
dim(neo_raw)
## [1] 42919    11
glimpse(neo_raw)
## Rows: 42,919
## Columns: 11
## $ neo_reference_id                  <chr> "2001620", "2001863", "2001915", "20…
## $ name                              <chr> "1620 Geographos (1951 RA)", "1863 A…
## $ absolute_magnitude_h              <dbl> 15.26, 15.35, 18.38, 14.38, 17.21, 1…
## $ est_diam_min_km                   <dbl> 2.3580607, 2.2623249, 0.5604734, 3.5…
## $ est_diam_max_km                   <dbl> 5.2727840, 5.0587123, 1.2532565, 7.9…
## $ miss_distance_km                  <dbl> 5071251, 13378706, 3789658, 16872242…
## $ relative_velocity_km_s            <dbl> 11.762812, 18.079514, 15.120986, 15.…
## $ orbital_eccentricity              <dbl> 0.3355122, 0.6062665, 0.5702246, 0.5…
## $ orbital_inclination               <dbl> 13.335793, 18.378651, 20.399239, 23.…
## $ orbital_semi_major_axis           <dbl> 1.2457769, 2.2601158, 2.5448412, 2.1…
## $ is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid <lgl> TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FA…

Data Cleaning

Step 1: Check for Missing Values

Before cleaning, we get a full picture of where data is missing. This tells us which variables have gaps and helps us decide whether to drop or keep those rows.

colSums(is.na(neo_raw))
##                  neo_reference_id                              name 
##                                 0                                 4 
##              absolute_magnitude_h                   est_diam_min_km 
##                                13                                13 
##                   est_diam_max_km                  miss_distance_km 
##                                13                              1762 
##            relative_velocity_km_s              orbital_eccentricity 
##                              1762                                 4 
##               orbital_inclination           orbital_semi_major_axis 
##                                 4                                 4 
## is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid 
##                                 0

Two groups of missing values stand out:

  • 1,762 rows are missing both miss_distance_km and relative_velocity_km_s. These are two of our most important predictor variables, so rows missing both cannot contribute meaningfully to modeling and will be removed.
  • A small number of rows (4–13) are missing diameter and orbital columns. These will also be dropped since imputing physical measurements for asteroids is not reliable.
neo_clean <- neo_raw %>%
  drop_na()

# Confirm no remaining missing values
colSums(is.na(neo_clean))
##                  neo_reference_id                              name 
##                                 0                                 0 
##              absolute_magnitude_h                   est_diam_min_km 
##                                 0                                 0 
##                   est_diam_max_km                  miss_distance_km 
##                                 0                                 0 
##            relative_velocity_km_s              orbital_eccentricity 
##                                 0                                 0 
##               orbital_inclination           orbital_semi_major_axis 
##                                 0                                 0 
## is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid 
##                                 0
nrow(neo_clean)
## [1] 41157

Step 2: Engineer a Mean Diameter Variable

The dataset provides minimum and maximum estimated diameter in kilometers. Rather than keeping both separately, we engineer a single diam_avg_km column as the midpoint between the two estimates. This gives us one clean numeric predictor representing size.

neo_clean <- neo_clean %>%
  mutate(diam_avg_km = (est_diam_min_km + est_diam_max_km) / 2)

Step 3: Convert the Target Variable to a Factor

The is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid column is currently stored as a logical (TRUE/FALSE). For modeling and plotting in R, it is better practice to convert this to a factor with meaningful labels. This also makes the variable behave correctly in ggplot2 legends and in classification models.

neo_clean <- neo_clean %>%
  mutate(hazardous = factor(
    is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid,
    levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
    labels = c("Not Hazardous", "Hazardous")
  )) %>%
  select(-is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid)  # remove original logical column

Step 4: Check for Duplicate Records

We verify there are no duplicate NEO IDs in the dataset. Duplicates could inflate model performance by leaking the same object into both training and test sets.

sum(duplicated(neo_clean$neo_reference_id))
## [1] 0

No duplicates found. Each row represents a unique NEO.

Step 5: Final Cleaned Dataset

glimpse(neo_clean)
## Rows: 41,157
## Columns: 12
## $ neo_reference_id        <chr> "2001620", "2001863", "2001915", "2001917", "2…
## $ name                    <chr> "1620 Geographos (1951 RA)", "1863 Antinous (1…
## $ absolute_magnitude_h    <dbl> 15.26, 15.35, 18.38, 14.38, 17.21, 16.24, 16.0…
## $ est_diam_min_km         <dbl> 2.3580607, 2.2623249, 0.5604734, 3.5363478, 0.…
## $ est_diam_max_km         <dbl> 5.2727840, 5.0587123, 1.2532565, 7.9075142, 2.…
## $ miss_distance_km        <dbl> 5071251, 13378706, 3789658, 16872242, 9959875,…
## $ relative_velocity_km_s  <dbl> 11.762812, 18.079514, 15.120986, 15.234490, 10…
## $ orbital_eccentricity    <dbl> 0.3355122, 0.6062665, 0.5702246, 0.5055378, 0.…
## $ orbital_inclination     <dbl> 13.335793, 18.378651, 20.399239, 23.958779, 9.…
## $ orbital_semi_major_axis <dbl> 1.2457769, 2.2601158, 2.5448412, 2.1490513, 1.…
## $ diam_avg_km             <dbl> 3.8154223, 3.6605186, 0.9068649, 5.7219310, 1.…
## $ hazardous               <fct> Hazardous, Not Hazardous, Not Hazardous, Not H…

Visualizations

Class Balance

neo_clean %>%
  count(hazardous) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = hazardous, y = n, fill = hazardous)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)), vjust = -0.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Class Distribution of Hazardous Label",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Distributions by Hazard Class

plot_df <- neo_clean %>%
  select(hazardous, diam_avg_km, relative_velocity_km_s, miss_distance_km) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(diam_avg_km, relative_velocity_km_s, miss_distance_km),
    names_to = "feature",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    feature = recode(
      feature,
      diam_avg_km = "Average Diameter (km)",
      relative_velocity_km_s = "Relative Velocity (km/s)",
      miss_distance_km = "Miss Distance (km)"
    )
  )

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = value, fill = hazardous, color = hazardous)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.25, linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free_x", ncol = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_number()) +
  labs(
    title = "Feature Distributions by Hazard Class (Log X Scale)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Density",
    fill = "Class",
    color = "Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Relative Velocity vs. Miss Distance

ggplot(
  neo_clean,
  aes(x = relative_velocity_km_s, y = miss_distance_km, color = hazardous)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.45, size = 1.6) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_number()) +
  labs(
    title = "Relative Velocity vs. Miss Distance",
    x = "Relative Velocity (km/s)",
    y = "Miss Distance (km, log scale)",
    color = "Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Correlation Heatmap (Numeric Predictors)

cor_df <- neo_clean %>%
  select(
    absolute_magnitude_h,
    est_diam_min_km,
    est_diam_max_km,
    diam_avg_km,
    miss_distance_km,
    relative_velocity_km_s,
    orbital_eccentricity,
    orbital_inclination,
    orbital_semi_major_axis
  )

cor_mat <- cor(cor_df, use = "complete.obs")

cor_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat)) %>%
  rename(var1 = Var1, var2 = Var2, correlation = Freq)

ggplot(cor_long, aes(x = var1, y = var2, fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#2166AC",
    mid = "white",
    high = "#B2182B",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits = c(-1, 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Correlation Heatmap of Numeric Predictors",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "r"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Variable Importance (Classification Tree)

tree_fit <- rpart(
  hazardous ~ diam_avg_km + relative_velocity_km_s + miss_distance_km +
    absolute_magnitude_h + orbital_eccentricity + orbital_inclination +
    orbital_semi_major_axis,
  data = neo_clean,
  method = "class"
)

importance_df <- tibble(
  variable = names(tree_fit$variable.importance),
  importance = as.numeric(tree_fit$variable.importance)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(importance))

ggplot(importance_df, aes(x = reorder(variable, importance), y = importance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1B9E77") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Variable Importance from Classification Tree",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

This first tree is an exploratory check rather than the final model evaluation. A proper held-out train/test comparison appears after the remaining research-question plots.

Orbital Characteristics by Hazard Class

neo_clean %>%
  select(hazardous, orbital_eccentricity, orbital_inclination, orbital_semi_major_axis) %>%
  # log10 transform semi-major axis before pivoting — raw values extend to 300+ AU
  # which collapses the box to nothing; log scale reveals the actual distribution
  mutate(orbital_semi_major_axis = log10(orbital_semi_major_axis)) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(orbital_eccentricity, orbital_inclination, orbital_semi_major_axis),
    names_to = "feature",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    feature = recode(
      feature,
      orbital_eccentricity    = "Orbital Eccentricity",
      orbital_inclination     = "Orbital Inclination (deg)",
      orbital_semi_major_axis = "Semi-Major Axis (log10 AU)"
    )
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = hazardous, y = value, fill = hazardous)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.15, outlier.size = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Orbital Characteristics by Hazard Classification",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Absolute Magnitude by Hazard Class

ggplot(neo_clean, aes(x = absolute_magnitude_h, fill = hazardous, color = hazardous)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.3, linewidth = 0.9) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Absolute Magnitude by Hazard Classification",
    x = "Absolute Magnitude (H)",
    y = "Density",
    fill = "Class",
    color = "Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(neo_clean, aes(x = hazardous, y = absolute_magnitude_h, fill = hazardous)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.2, outlier.size = 0.8, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Absolute Magnitude by Hazard Classification",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Absolute Magnitude (H)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Pairwise Scatterplots of Top Predictors

var_pairs <- list(
  list(x = "diam_avg_km",          y = "absolute_magnitude_h"),
  list(x = "diam_avg_km",          y = "orbital_eccentricity"),
  list(x = "diam_avg_km",          y = "orbital_inclination"),
  list(x = "absolute_magnitude_h", y = "orbital_eccentricity"),
  list(x = "absolute_magnitude_h", y = "orbital_inclination"),
  list(x = "orbital_eccentricity", y = "orbital_inclination")
)

pair_df <- bind_rows(lapply(var_pairs, function(p) {
  neo_clean %>%
    transmute(
      hazardous,
      x_val   = .data[[p$x]],
      y_val   = .data[[p$y]],
      panel   = paste(p$x, "vs", p$y)
    )
}))

ggplot(pair_df, aes(x = x_val, y = y_val, color = hazardous)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ panel, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Pairwise Scatterplots of Top Four Predictors",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    color = "Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text      = element_text(size = 7),
    axis.text       = element_text(size = 6)
  )

Hazard Rate by Asteroid Size

size_hazard_summary <- neo_clean %>%
  mutate(
    size_group = cut(
      diam_avg_km,
      breaks = c(0, 0.03, 0.10, 0.30, 1.00, Inf),
      labels = c("< 0.03", "0.03-0.10", "0.10-0.30", "0.30-1.00", "1.00+"),
      right = FALSE
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(size_group) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    hazardous_n = sum(hazardous == "Hazardous"),
    hazard_rate = hazardous_n / n,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

size_hazard_summary %>%
  mutate(hazard_rate = format_percent(hazard_rate)) %>%
  rename(
    `Average Diameter Group (km)` = size_group,
    `Asteroid Count` = n,
    `Hazardous Count` = hazardous_n,
    `Percent Hazardous` = hazard_rate
  ) %>%
  kable(caption = "Hazard rate by average diameter group")
Hazard rate by average diameter group
Average Diameter Group (km) Asteroid Count Hazardous Count Percent Hazardous
< 0.03 10381 0 0.0%
0.03-0.10 14705 0 0.0%
0.10-0.30 8708 1178 13.5%
0.30-1.00 6164 1315 21.3%
1.00+ 1199 230 19.2% 
ggplot(size_hazard_summary, aes(x = size_group, y = hazard_rate)) +
  geom_col(fill = accent_color, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = scales::percent(hazard_rate, accuracy = 0.1)),
    vjust = -0.35,
    size = 3.4
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, max(size_hazard_summary$hazard_rate) * 1.15)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Hazard Rate by Average Diameter Group",
    x = "Average Diameter Group (km)",
    y = "Percent Hazardous"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The two smallest diameter groups have a 0.0% hazardous rate. The hazardous rate rises sharply once average diameter reaches 0.10-0.30 km, peaks in the 0.30-1.00 km group, and stays high for asteroids above 1.00 km. This supports the presentation takeaway that NASA’s hazardous label is strongly connected to asteroid size.

Diameter and Absolute Magnitude Together

magnitude_diameter_corr <- cor(
  neo_clean$absolute_magnitude_h,
  neo_clean$diam_avg_km,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

tibble(
  relationship = "Absolute magnitude H vs. average diameter",
  correlation = round(magnitude_diameter_corr, 3)
) %>%
  kable(caption = "Correlation between absolute magnitude and average diameter")
Correlation between absolute magnitude and average diameter
relationship correlation
Absolute magnitude H vs. average diameter -0.523 
ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = filter(neo_clean, hazardous == "Not Hazardous"),
    aes(x = absolute_magnitude_h, y = diam_avg_km, color = hazardous),
    alpha = 0.12,
    size = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = filter(neo_clean, hazardous == "Hazardous"),
    aes(x = absolute_magnitude_h, y = diam_avg_km, color = hazardous),
    alpha = 0.7,
    size = 1.1
  ) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_number()) +
  scale_color_manual(values = class_colors) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Diameter vs. Absolute Magnitude by Hazard Class",
    x = "Absolute Magnitude H",
    y = "Average Diameter (km, log scale)",
    color = "Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Average diameter and absolute magnitude move together very strongly: lower absolute magnitude generally means a brighter and larger asteroid. That is why both variables show up as important in the analysis. For interpretation, they should be treated as overlapping evidence about size rather than as two independent findings.

Modeling

We use a classification tree because the earlier plots suggest threshold-like behavior: very small asteroids are rarely labeled hazardous, while larger objects are much more likely to be flagged. To keep the evaluation honest, the data is split into stratified training and test sets before fitting the models.

The model uses the main orbital, close-approach, size, and brightness predictors from the exploratory analysis.

model_vars_full <- c(
  "absolute_magnitude_h",
  "diam_avg_km",
  "miss_distance_km",
  "relative_velocity_km_s",
  "orbital_eccentricity",
  "orbital_inclination",
  "orbital_semi_major_axis"
)

set.seed(2026)

neo_model <- neo_clean %>%
  select(neo_reference_id, hazardous, all_of(model_vars_full)) %>%
  mutate(row_id = row_number())

train_ids <- neo_model %>%
  group_by(hazardous) %>%
  slice_sample(prop = 0.80) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  pull(row_id)

train_data <- neo_model %>%
  filter(row_id %in% train_ids) %>%
  select(-row_id, -neo_reference_id)

test_data <- neo_model %>%
  filter(!row_id %in% train_ids) %>%
  select(-row_id, -neo_reference_id)

tibble(
  split = c("Train", "Test"),
  rows = c(nrow(train_data), nrow(test_data)),
  hazardous_rate = c(
    mean(train_data$hazardous == "Hazardous"),
    mean(test_data$hazardous == "Hazardous")
  )
) %>%
  mutate(hazardous_rate = format_percent(hazardous_rate)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Stratified train/test split")
Stratified train/test split
split rows hazardous_rate
Train 32925 6.6%
Test 8232 6.6%

Evaluation Functions

safe_divide <- function(numerator, denominator) {
  ifelse(denominator == 0, NA_real_, numerator / denominator)
}

confusion_metrics <- function(actual, predicted, positive = "Hazardous") {
  actual <- as.character(actual)
  predicted <- as.character(predicted)
  negative <- "Not Hazardous"

  tp <- sum(actual == positive & predicted == positive)
  tn <- sum(actual == negative & predicted == negative)
  fp <- sum(actual == negative & predicted == positive)
  fn <- sum(actual == positive & predicted == negative)

  tibble(
    tp = tp,
    tn = tn,
    fp = fp,
    fn = fn,
    accuracy = safe_divide(tp + tn, tp + tn + fp + fn),
    recall = safe_divide(tp, tp + fn),
    specificity = safe_divide(tn, tn + fp),
    precision = safe_divide(tp, tp + fp)
  )
}

Fit Classification Tree

full_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("hazardous ~", paste(model_vars_full, collapse = " + "))
)

tree_full <- rpart(
  full_formula,
  data = train_data,
  method = "class",
  control = rpart.control(cp = 0.001, minbucket = 25)
)

tree_full_predicted <- predict(tree_full, newdata = test_data, type = "class")

Results

Classification Tree Performance

model_metrics <- confusion_metrics(test_data$hazardous, tree_full_predicted) %>%
  select(
    accuracy,
    recall,
    specificity,
    precision,
    tp,
    tn,
    fp,
    fn
  )

model_metrics %>%
  mutate(
    across(c(accuracy, recall, specificity, precision), ~ format_percent(.x))
  ) %>%
  select(
    Accuracy = accuracy,
    Recall = recall,
    Specificity = specificity,
    Precision = precision,
    `True Positives` = tp,
    `True Negatives` = tn,
    `False Positives` = fp,
    `False Negatives` = fn
  ) %>%
  kable(caption = "Classification tree performance on the test data")
Classification tree performance on the test data
Accuracy Recall Specificity Precision True Positives True Negatives False Positives False Negatives
94.2% 32.5% 98.6% 61.7% 177 7577 110 368

The tree has high overall accuracy, but recall and precision are more important because the hazardous class is small. The confusion matrix below shows how many hazardous asteroids the model catches and misses.

Confusion Matrix Heatmap

confusion_df <- tibble(
  actual = test_data$hazardous,
  predicted = tree_full_predicted
) %>%
  count(actual, predicted) %>%
  complete(
    actual = levels(test_data$hazardous),
    predicted = levels(test_data$hazardous),
    fill = list(n = 0)
  )

ggplot(confusion_df, aes(x = predicted, y = actual, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#C6DBEF", high = class_colors[["Not Hazardous"]]) +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrix: Classification Tree",
    x = "Predicted Class",
    y = "Actual Class",
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Classification Tree Variable Importance

importance_df <- tibble(
  variable = names(tree_full$variable.importance),
  importance = as.numeric(tree_full$variable.importance)
) %>%
  mutate(variable = recode(variable, !!!feature_labels)) %>%
  arrange(desc(importance))

importance_df %>%
  mutate(importance = round(importance, 1)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Most important variables in the full classification tree")
Most important variables in the full classification tree
variable importance
Absolute Magnitude H 731.0
Average Diameter (km) 730.4
Orbital Inclination 313.2
Orbital Eccentricity 234.7
Relative Velocity (km/s) 229.7
Miss Distance (km) 203.6
Orbital Semi-Major Axis 197.4 
ggplot(importance_df, aes(x = reorder(variable, importance), y = importance)) +
  geom_col(fill = accent_color) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Variable Importance from Full Classification Tree",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The model results reinforce the visual findings: size and brightness are central to the hazardous label, while the other orbital and close-approach variables provide weaker separation. The model is useful as an explanatory check, but it should not be interpreted as a real-world asteroid warning system.

Conclusion

The strongest pattern in the data is that NASA’s hazardous label is closely tied to asteroid size and brightness. Hazardous asteroids are rare in the cleaned dataset, so accuracy alone is misleading. The classification tree helps summarize which variables matter most, but the model should be interpreted as an explanatory check rather than a real-world warning system.

Future work should add Earth MOID, the official orbital-distance part of the PHA definition, and should compare multiple close approaches for the same asteroid rather than relying on one summarized approach field. It would also be useful to test whether one of diameter or absolute magnitude can be dropped without losing much predictive performance.
